title: The Bookstore

jokes.text: So, what's the deal with politics? I don't get it. Am I right, people?
jokes.setup: Jerry doing his standup routine
jokes.punchline: Generic comedian opener that parodies his own style
jokes.explanation: Self-aware meta-joke about Jerry's comedy style - very character-specific and well-executed parody

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Generic comedian opener that parodies his own style

jokes.text: They can put it anywhere they want. No one's meeting anybody.
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining bookstore as pickup location
jokes.punchline: Jerry's cynical dismissal of dating advice trends
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry cynicism delivered with perfect economy - very character-specific worldview

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A bookstore? What am I gonna do, stand in the poetry section and wait for someone to ask me for a haiku? 'Excuse me, do you know where the self-help books are?' Yeah, I'm looking for the one that explains why I'm talking to you.

jokes.text: Yes. The city needs more slow moving wicker vehicles.
jokes.setup: Kramer suggesting rickshaws for New York
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sarcastic assessment highlighting the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Perfect sarcastic response that captures the impracticality - highly quotable and economical

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's a great idea. Let's bring rickshaws to New York. What could possibly go wrong? We'll have guys running around in the heat pulling tourists. That's not going to end badly at all.

jokes.text: Forgot his first name.
jokes.setup: George asking who Leo is, Jerry explaining it's Uncle Leo
jokes.punchline: Jerry's casual admission of forgetting his own uncle's name
jokes.explanation: Simple family dysfunction humor - economical but not particularly original

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Who's Leo?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's my Uncle Leo.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Your uncle? How could you forget your own uncle's name?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I don't see him that often.

jokes.text: Maybe. All over your face.
jokes.setup: Someone asking if Elaine will dance this year
jokes.punchline: Elaine's aggressive non-sequitur response
jokes.explanation: Elaine's characteristic aggressive response - fits her confrontational personality well

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm out there, Jerry, and I'm loving every minute of it!

jokes.text: Hi. I'm miserable.
jokes.setup: Man introducing himself as Zach at party
jokes.punchline: Elaine's brutally honest introduction
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of social pleasantries with brutal honesty - extremely economical and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Zach
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Zach.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm Elaine, and you're making a huge mistake.

jokes.text: If it wasn't for the toilet, there would be no books.
jokes.setup: George defending taking book to bathroom
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous philosophical claim about toilet-reading relationship
jokes.explanation: Quintessential George logic - absurd but delivered with complete conviction. Highly quotable and original

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not ridiculous! A man reads a book in the bathroom, it's like a sacred bond between him and the book. You're alone, you're vulnerable, you're at your most honest.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, it's a book. Not a person.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly! That's why it works. A book doesn't judge you. A book accepts you for who you are.

jokes.text: Yeah, I understand Gutenberg used to spend a fair amount of time in there.
jokes.setup: George's claim about toilets and books
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dry historical reference extending the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Clever historical callback that plays along with George's absurd logic while maintaining Jerry's dry wit

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, the toilet is the most important invention of our time. You can sit there and read a book for hours.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, Gutenberg had the same idea. He just called it the printing press.

jokes.text: All right, Leo. Sticking it to the man.
jokes.setup: Jerry learning Uncle Leo was shoplifting
jokes.punchline: Jerry's ironic praise for elderly rebellion
jokes.explanation: Good ironic response that contrasts Uncle Leo's petty crime with revolutionary rhetoric

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So Uncle Leo's a shoplifter. That's great. That's just what I needed to hear. My uncle, the criminal. I'm very proud.

jokes.text: Cocktail flu.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking why Elaine is sleeping in car
jokes.punchline: Elaine's euphemism for hangover
jokes.explanation: Clever euphemism for hangover - economical and mildly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why are you sleeping in my car?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm not sleeping, I'm just resting my eyes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You've been in there for three hours.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm tired. I had a very active evening last night.

jokes.text: Oh, the drunken make-out. An office classic.
jokes.setup: Elaine describing her embarrassing party behavior
jokes.punchline: Jerry categorizing it as a common workplace occurrence
jokes.explanation: Jerry's clinical categorization of embarrassing behavior - good observational humor

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I can't believe I did that at the party. I was dancing on the table and everyone saw my underwear.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's a classic move. You've entered the pantheon of party legends.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not funny, Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, it's actually very common. There's a whole workplace category for this: 'Dancing on furniture while intoxicated.' It's right up there with 'accidentally hitting send on an email to your boss.'

jokes.text: Did you end up Xeroxing anything?
jokes.setup: Discussion of office party antics
jokes.punchline: Jerry asking about stereotypical drunk office behavior
jokes.explanation: Reference to common office party trope - functional but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So at office parties, there's always that one guy who gets drunk and starts doing things he shouldn't be doing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, like hitting on the boss's wife or telling everyone what he really thinks about them.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why is that? Why does alcohol give people permission to be themselves?

jokes.text: Hey, me too. / Yeah, me too.
jokes.setup: Elaine saying she's an associate
jokes.punchline: Jerry and George claiming the same meaningless title
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation joke about meaningless titles - works but is fairly basic

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Associate? What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm an associate at my office.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're an associate? I'm an associate too!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, what do you associate with?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I associate with you, and you associate with me. We're both associates.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is great. We should get business cards made up.

jokes.text: As opposed to a spirited bout of skanko Roman wrestling.
jokes.setup: Discussing how to reframe Elaine's public make-out session
jokes.punchline: Zach's creative metaphor for sloppy drunken behavior
jokes.explanation: Highly original and vivid metaphor - 'skanko Roman wrestling' is brilliant wordplay and imagery

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What am I going to do? Everyone saw me making out with that guy!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You just need to reframe it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Reframe it how?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Tell people you were practicing CPR.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: On his tonsils?

jokes.text: No, you're not. / No, he doesn't. / Yes, it can.
jokes.setup: Kramer making increasingly grandiose claims about rickshaw business
jokes.punchline: Jerry's systematic deflation of each claim
jokes.explanation: Perfect rhythm of systematic deflation - excellent structure and character dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, this rickshaw business is going to be huge. I'm thinking about expanding to other cities.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't even have a rickshaw.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, not yet. But I've got connections.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What connections? Who do you know in the rickshaw industry?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: People. I know people.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know people. Okay. And these people, they're in rickshaws?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: They could be.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They could be. So you're basing your entire business plan on the possibility that people you may or may not know might possibly be involved in rickshaws.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, you should be more supportive.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I am supportive. I'm supporting you right out of the rickshaw business.

jokes.text: My, isn't this an awkward moment?
jokes.setup: Kramer and Newman realizing they both expected the other to pull the rickshaw
jokes.punchline: Newman acknowledging the awkwardness directly
jokes.explanation: Newman's characteristic way of addressing uncomfortable situations - fits his character well

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well? I'm waiting.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I'm waiting.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What do you mean you're waiting? I thought you were pulling!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I thought YOU were pulling!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: This is awkward.

jokes.text: Why not just strap something to them?
jokes.setup: Discussion of homeless people pulling rickshaws
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual dehumanization through practical suggestion
jokes.explanation: Dark humor from Kramer's oblivious perspective - morally questionable but character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, if homeless people pulled rickshaws, they'd have a purpose. They'd be part of the economy.

jokes.text: Now, that's the first sensible idea I've heard all day.
jokes.setup: Kramer suggesting strapping things to homeless people
jokes.punchline: Newman's enthusiastic endorsement of the dehumanizing idea
jokes.explanation: Newman's moral flexibility on display - darkly funny character consistency

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Yeah! We could strap a little advertising sign to them. 'Newman's fruit company.' They get a few bucks, we get some free advertising!

jokes.text: But I'm not.
jokes.setup: Leo saying he should be loaning Jerry money
jokes.punchline: Leo's matter-of-fact clarification that he won't
jokes.explanation: Leo's blunt honesty about not helping family - economical and character-specific

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: I should be loaning you money.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: But I'm not going to.

jokes.text: You still say hello.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining he didn't say hello because Leo was stealing
jokes.punchline: Leo's insistence on proper etiquette during criminal activity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Leo's priorities are hilariously skewed toward social niceties over legality

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: What? I was just borrowing it!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You were stealing my newspaper!

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: That's no reason not to say hello. A simple 'hello' costs nothing.

jokes.text: Who, criminals? / Senior citizens. No big deal.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking who 'we all do it' refers to
jokes.punchline: Leo's casual categorization of elderly shoplifting as normal
jokes.explanation: Leo's matter-of-fact attitude toward senior crime - great character-specific worldview

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: Well, you know, we all do it. At our age, it's expected.

jokes.text: I'm an old man. I'm confused. I thought I paid for it. What's my name? Could you take me home?
jokes.setup: Leo explaining his shoplifting defense strategy
jokes.punchline: Leo's rehearsed helpless old man routine
jokes.explanation: Leo's calculated manipulation of old age stereotypes - good escalating performance

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: You see, I go into the store, I'm shuffling around, I can barely walk. I'm bumping into displays, knocking things over. The manager comes running over, 'Mr. Leo, can I help you?' I say, 'I can't find the prunes.' He's so busy helping me look for prunes, he doesn't even notice I've got three cashmere sweaters under my coat.

jokes.text: Will somebody answer that damn phone.
jokes.setup: Leo mentioned earlier he has ringing in his ears that sounds like a phone
jokes.punchline: Leo getting frustrated with his own tinnitus
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Leo's medical condition - works as character continuity

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: Hello? Hello? Is anybody there? I can't hear you, the line's breaking up.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: What? What did you say? You're gonna have to speak up!

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: Oh, forget it! This is ridiculous!

jokes.text: Now, I don't care where you're from, or how you got here, or what happened to your homes, but you will have to be physically fit.
jokes.setup: Kramer interviewing homeless people for rickshaw jobs
jokes.punchline: Kramer's callous dismissal of their circumstances while demanding fitness
jokes.explanation: Kramer's tone-deaf business approach - darkly funny but character-consistent obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I need someone with real hustle, real drive. You gotta be in top physical condition. I'm not gonna have some out-of-shape bum pulling my rickshaw.

jokes.text: or a shirt.
jokes.setup: Kramer listing physical requirements for rickshaw pullers
jokes.punchline: Kramer's afterthought about basic clothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing - the casual addition of basic human dignity as an afterthought

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, you gotta be very strong. You gotta have great leg muscles, a powerful heart... And you can't be afraid of death.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What about clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, yeah, you gotta have some kind of loincloth situation going.

jokes.text: You know, I once knew a horse named Rusty. No offense.
jokes.setup: Homeless man introducing himself as Rusty
jokes.punchline: Kramer's inappropriate comparison to a horse
jokes.explanation: Kramer's oblivious insensitivity - comparing human to work animal while hiring for manual labor

dialogue.speaker: Homeless Man
dialogue.line: I'm Rusty.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Rusty? That's a nice name. You know, I had a horse named Rusty once.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: He was a big, strong animal... had a beautiful coat... you remind me of him.

jokes.text: I'll take the job. Potato salad.
jokes.setup: Rickshaw being stolen, need for new employee
jokes.punchline: Non-sequitur food reference during job interview
jokes.explanation: Random = funny attempt - doesn't earn the laugh, feels cheap and disconnected

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So you're interested in the position?

dialogue.speaker: Homeless man
dialogue.line: Yeah, I need work.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, it's simple. You pull the rickshaw, customers sit in back, you get paid.

dialogue.speaker: Homeless man
dialogue.line: Sounds good. By the way, you got any pastrami?

jokes.text: It's been in the bathroom. / It says that on the computer?
jokes.setup: George trying to return the book he took to bathroom
jokes.punchline: Revelation that bathroom books are tracked in computer system
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - the idea that bookstores track bathroom contamination is both absurd and believable

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'd like to return this book.

dialogue.speaker: Bookstore clerk
dialogue.line: Do you have your receipt?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I don't have a receipt.

dialogue.speaker: Bookstore clerk
dialogue.line: Well, let me just scan it here and see what comes up.

dialogue.speaker: Bookstore clerk
dialogue.line: Hmm, that's odd. It says here this book was taken to the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? How could you possibly know that?

dialogue.speaker: Bookstore clerk
dialogue.line: It's all in the computer system. We track everything.

jokes.text: 5150. That's just a scare, right?
jokes.setup: Security calling in code for Uncle Leo
jokes.punchline: Jerry's growing concern about the seriousness
jokes.explanation: Good escalation as Jerry realizes his plan backfired - solid structure

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Code Blue, we have a Code Blue for Leo Costanza in the lobby.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, Code Blue? That's a heart attack!

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: No, no. He's just blocking the revolving door.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh thank God... Wait, why is that a Code Blue?

jokes.text: Jerry, you ratted me out.
jokes.setup: Leo being arrested after Jerry asked security to scare him
jokes.punchline: Leo's accusation using criminal terminology
jokes.explanation: Leo embracing criminal identity while blaming Jerry - good character moment and escalation

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: You set me up! You're in cahoots with security!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Uncle Leo, I just asked them to scare you a little.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: Scare me? You conspired against me! This is a setup, a frame job!

jokes.text: It was a crime of passion. Leave it alone.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Uncle Leo's criminal past
jokes.punchline: Helen's dismissive reference to serious crime as if it's romantic
jokes.explanation: Helen's casual dismissal of violent crime as romantic - great character-specific moral flexibility

dialogue.speaker: Helen Seinfeld
dialogue.line: Oh, don't be silly. Uncle Leo never robbed anyone. He was just in the wrong place at the wrong time.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Seinfeld
dialogue.line: Mom, he was arrested three times.

dialogue.speaker: Helen Seinfeld
dialogue.line: So? A man with a past is a man with character. It's romantic!

jokes.text: Batteries. Well, they wear out so quick. / Sometimes your father forgets, so, I have to steal them.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking what his mother steals
jokes.punchline: Helen's practical justification for petty theft
jokes.explanation: Helen's matter-of-fact approach to shoplifting - good family dysfunction humor

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Seinfeld
dialogue.line: Ma, why do you steal things?

dialogue.speaker: Helen Seinfeld
dialogue.line: What do you mean steal? I'm not stealing. I'm just taking things that I need.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Seinfeld
dialogue.line: You need a monogrammed towel from a hotel?

dialogue.speaker: Helen Seinfeld
dialogue.line: It's a good towel! What am I gonna do, leave it there for them to use again? It's already been used!

jokes.text: Well, you know, 85 percent of all homeless-rickshaw businesses fail within the first three months.
jokes.setup: Learning their rickshaw business failed when homeless man stole the rickshaw
jokes.punchline: Jerry's fake statistic treating absurd business as legitimate industry
jokes.explanation: Brilliant fake statistic that treats ridiculous concept as established business model - highly quotable and original

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, rickshaw operators have the highest suicide rate of any profession.

jokes.text: Like his bag of cans, or his other bag of cans.
jokes.setup: Discussion of getting collateral from homeless employee
jokes.punchline: Newman's recognition that homeless possessions are limited to cans
jokes.explanation: Dark observation about homelessness with good escalation structure

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: What are you gonna take? His cans?

jokes.text: To the idiot-mobile.
jokes.setup: Newman and Kramer heading out to search for rickshaw
jokes.punchline: Newman's self-aware parody of heroic departure
jokes.explanation: Newman's rare moment of self-awareness - good parody of superhero tropes

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: This is it, Kramer. We ride at dawn.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: We'll search every corner of this city until we find that rickshaw. Nothing will stop us. Nothing!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I can see it now... two heroes, bound by fate, destined to recover what was lost.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's beautiful, man.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Of course, I'll probably be winded after two blocks, but the sentiment is there.

jokes.text: Tina Turner?
jokes.setup: Elaine saying she'll be office hero for standing up for herself
jokes.punchline: Jerry's non-sequitur pop culture reference
jokes.explanation: Weak pop culture reference without clear connection or twist - feels lazy

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, that reminds me of the time Superman saved that cat from a tree.

jokes.text: Anyone betrays me, I never forget.
jokes.setup: Leo referencing his crime of passion
jokes.punchline: Leo's ominous threat delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Leo's casual menace - great character moment showing his dark side beneath elderly facade

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: You know, I killed a man once. It was a crime of passion. I found him with my wife.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: If I ever find another man with my wife, I'll kill him too.

jokes.text: You know, I could let it go for say a hundred and twenty-five.
jokes.setup: George unable to return the book, now trying to sell it
jokes.punchline: George trying to profit from his contaminated book
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - turning his problem into a get-rich-quick scheme. Classic character behavior

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Look, I'll sell it to you for five bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Bookstore Employee
dialogue.line: We can't sell this book. It's been damaged.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Damaged? It's got character! It's got history! Someone's gonna want this book.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How about three bucks?

jokes.text: Biohazard, coming through. Clear. Clear.
jokes.setup: The contaminated book being brought to the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Kramer's overdramatic hazmat response
jokes.explanation: Kramer's characteristic overreaction - good physical comedy and escalation

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A book? A book came in here contaminated?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: We're gonna need a quarantine. We're gonna need to get some sort of... some sort of containment system set up here.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: This whole place is a biohazard! We could all be infected!

jokes.text: Well, aren't you something?
jokes.setup: Jerry claiming he doesn't read in the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Elaine's sarcastic response to Jerry's supposed superiority
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic comeback - functional but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, so you're too sophisticated to read in the bathroom? What, you just sit there and think about world events?

jokes.text: Who among us hasn't snuck into the break room to nibble on a love newton?
jokes.setup: Peterman defending office romance behavior
jokes.punchline: Peterman's poetic euphemism for workplace romance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Peterman flowery language - 'love newton' is brilliantly absurd and character-specific

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: The relationship between a man and a woman in the workplace is not about romance, it's about the poetry of human connection. It's about two people finding solace in the fluorescent-lit corridors of commerce.

jokes.text: Smack. White palace. The Chinamen's nightcap.
jokes.setup: Peterman explaining that Zach is a drug addict
jokes.punchline: Peterman's colorful synonyms for heroin
jokes.explanation: Classic Peterman elaborate terminology - the escalating euphemisms are perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: He's addicted to heroin. Horse. Smack. The devil's candy. Nose clams. Snooze buttons.

jokes.text: That wasn't Zach. That was the yam-yam.
jokes.setup: Elaine saying Zach was cheating on her
jokes.punchline: Peterman's drug nickname explanation for infidelity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Peterman moment - 'yam-yam' as drug slang is absurd and the explanation is perfect character voice

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: You know, in my day, we had a name for men like that. We called them 'Scum.' But that was before my time in the Amazon. While I was down there, I picked up some local parlance. They have a word for infidelity... it's 'Zach.'

jokes.text: Better bring a poncho.
jokes.setup: Peterman warning about the first 24 hours of withdrawal
jokes.punchline: Peterman's casual practical advice about bodily fluids
jokes.explanation: Perfect Peterman practical advice delivered with characteristic nonchalance about disturbing details

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: The first 24 hours of withdrawal are the worst. You'll experience violent mood swings, tremors, and an uncontrollable urge to urinate.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: My advice? Wear dark pants.

jokes.text: Away from the curtains. Away. Use your bucket.
jokes.setup: Elaine dealing with Zach's withdrawal symptoms
jokes.punchline: Elaine's matter-of-fact directions for drug withdrawal cleanup
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Elaine's practical response to horrifying situation

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: First, you gotta get the stain out of the carpet. Then you need to call a plumber because the pipes are gonna be all clogged up with... stuff. And whatever you do, don't use the shower for at least a week.

jokes.text: I mean, after all, it is a conveyance.
jokes.setup: Newman and Kramer both pulling the rickshaw
jokes.punchline: Newman's sophisticated justification for laziness
jokes.explanation: Newman's attempt to sound refined while avoiding work - good character moment

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I'm not lazy, Kramer. I'm energy efficient.

jokes.text: One spot, two spot, zigzag, tear, pop-dye, penny got, tennyum, tear, harum-scarum, rip them, tear them, te, ta, toe.
jokes.setup: Newman and Kramer deciding who pulls rickshaw
jokes.punchline: Newman's elaborate nonsense counting rhyme
jokes.explanation: Absurdist Newman moment but too long and random - loses comedic momentum

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Eeny, meeny, miny, moe, catch a tiger by the toe. If he hollers, let him go, eeny, meeny, miny, moe.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I'm counting out! You pull the rickshaw.

jokes.text: Smooth it out up there. Too much jostling.
jokes.setup: Newman riding in rickshaw while Kramer pulls
jokes.punchline: Newman's demanding customer complaints
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to Newman avoiding work - his entitlement as a passenger is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Faster! Faster! I'm going to be late for my shift at the post office!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm pulling as fast as I can, Newman!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Well, pull faster! This is unacceptable! I could have walked faster than this!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: And another thing - it's too bumpy! Smooth it out!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Newman, there's only so much I can do here!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: That's what I'm talking about! No initiative! No drive! You're fired!

jokes.text: It's DeMornay. Rebecca DeMornay.
jokes.setup: George mispronouncing celebrity name at charity
jokes.punchline: Celebrity's correction and full name introduction
jokes.explanation: Standard celebrity cameo moment - functional but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So Rebecca DeMornay is here tonight, right?

dialogue.speaker: Rebecca DeMornay
dialogue.line: It's DeMornay. De-Mor-nay.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's what I said.

dialogue.speaker: Rebecca DeMornay
dialogue.line: No, you said DeMorney. I'm Rebecca DeMornay.

jokes.text: It's bad enough that we have some nut out there trying to strap them to a rickshaw.
jokes.setup: Discussion of helping homeless people
jokes.punchline: Reference to Kramer and Newman's rickshaw scheme
jokes.explanation: Good callback connecting storylines - shows the wider impact of Kramer's scheme

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, we're supposed to help every homeless person we see?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not equipped for this. I can barely help myself.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Actually, I've got a business idea. Newman and I are starting a rickshaw service.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A rickshaw service? With Newman?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, we pull people around the city. It's good exercise, we help the environment, and we make money.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's the worst idea I've ever heard.

jokes.text: You get your toilet book out of here and I won't jump over this counter and punch you in the brain.
jokes.setup: George trying to donate his contaminated book
jokes.punchline: Celebrity's shocking threat of violence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - glamorous celebrity threatening specific violence over book contamination. Highly quotable

jokes.text: You're better at fake relationships than real ones. / Yeah. I even got an idea out of it. The detox poncho.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Elaine's fake relationship success
jokes.punchline: Elaine turning horrible experience into business opportunity
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Elaine's entrepreneurial spirit finding opportunity in trauma. Good callback

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about this whole thing. All those bad dates, all those terrible relationships... I could turn this into something.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Turn it into what?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: A business! I'll write a book: 'The Relationship Disaster Guide.' Or maybe seminars. 'How to Date Badly and Live to Tell About It.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're going to profit off your own misery?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Exactly! Everyone else does. Why shouldn't I?

jokes.text: Well, don't tarry, I'm behind schedule as it is.
jokes.setup: Kramer taking a break from pulling the rickshaw
jokes.punchline: Newman's entitled demands despite contributing nothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Newman entitlement - using formal language while being completely unreasonable

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I'm exhausted! I need a break!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You? You're not even pulling anything!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Well, I'm supervising. That's very demanding work.

jokes.text: You defile one book, steal another, ask for your money back and to you that's even.
jokes.setup: George explaining his plan to steal a book to return
jokes.punchline: Jerry's summary of George's twisted logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry observation of George's moral mathematics - good structure and character dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So let me get this straight. You're gonna steal a book, and then return it to the library?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's right.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And that way you get to read it for free, and the library thinks you found it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly!

jokes.text: That's why we stopped carrying batteries.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying all old people steal
jokes.punchline: Store's policy response to elderly shoplifting
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Jerry's mother's battery theft - shows the broader impact with perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: Bookstore manager
dialogue.line: We have a special senior citizen discount.

dialogue.speaker: Bookstore manager
dialogue.line: It's called 'shoplift and get 20% off.'

jokes.text: Oh, Jerry!
jokes.setup: Jerry pointing out George shoplifting to get Uncle Leo off the hook
jokes.punchline: Leo's recognition that Jerry has betrayed someone else
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback ending - Leo's disapproval of Jerry's pattern of betrayal brings his arc full circle

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Leo
dialogue.line: You threw me under the bus!

tmdb_overview: A bookstore busts Uncle Leo for shoplifting and George for bathroom reading; a misdeed at a company party costs Elaine; Kramer and Newman get a rickshaw, and audition homeless people to pull it.
insight: Bookstore tension escalates through character-driven absurdism at 3 jokes-per-minute clip.
